title: `PortMappings can only be used with Bridge, slirp4netns, or pasta networking` when using `Network=private` with pod
body:
### Issue Description

Using Quadlet to run a Kubernetes pod, the unit file contains this:

```
[Kube]
Network=private
```

According to the [docs](https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-kube-play.1.html), using `private` should create a new namespace for the container(s):

> private: Create a new namespace for the container. This uses the bridge mode for rootful containers and slirp4netns for rootless ones.

However, if the Pod YAML contains definitions to expose ports, the unit fails to start with error:

> PortMappings can only be used with Bridge, slirp4netns, or pasta networking


### Steps to reproduce the issue

See issue description

### Describe the results you received

Pod fails to start with "PortMappings can only be used with Bridge, slirp4netns, or pasta networking"

### Describe the results you expected

Pod starts correctly. Since "private" networks create a new bridge network, it should work as expected.

comments.body: Do you have any containers.conf options set, most likely you overwrite the default `netns` option there.

> Do you have any containers.conf options set, most likely you overwrite the default `netns` option there.

Nope

```
$ cat /etc/containers/containers.conf
[network]
network_backend="cni"
```
Please provide your k8s yaml file

I am not able to provide the YAML file, but at its core it's just a Pod with nothing special for the network - aside from exposing one hostPort

The quadlet unit is:

```
[Unit]
Description=Server
Requires=network-online.target
After=network-online.target
# Allow unlimited restarts
StartLimitIntervalSec=0

[Service]
Restart=always
RestartSec=2s

[Kube]
# This is the issue
Network=private
Yaml=/etc/apps/server.yaml
AutoUpdate=registry

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target default.target
```
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I ran into the same error using `Network=host` with this:

```
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target default.target

[Kube]
Yaml=candlepin.yaml
LogDriver=journald
Network=host

PublishPort=8443:8443
PublishPort=61613:61613
```

> I ran into the same error using `Network=host` with this:
> 
> ```
> [Install]
> WantedBy=multi-user.target default.target
> 
> [Kube]
> Yaml=candlepin.yaml
> LogDriver=journald
> Network=host
> 
> PublishPort=8443:8443
> PublishPort=61613:61613
> ```

Well yeah you cannot forward ports when using the host network mode so remove the ports from there.

> Well yeah you cannot forward ports when using the host network mode so remove the ports from there.

What is unclear is where to then put those ports so that they are exposed. This was not obvious from the docs or any of the blog posts, especially when going from a `.container` based set to a `.kube` based setup for quadlets. I was able after some trial and error to figure out that I needed to put those port mappings into the Pod definition as part of the supplied `Yaml=candlepin.yaml` file. 

I have tried to capture this in a PR that would have saved me time -- https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/22242

> What is unclear is where to then put those ports so that they are exposed. This was not obvious from the docs or any of the blog posts, especially when going from a .container based set to a .kube based setup for quadlets. I was able after some trial and error to figure out that I needed to put those port mappings into the Pod definition as part of the supplied Yaml=candlepin.yaml file.

That makes no sense, if you use host networking podman doesn't touch anything with ports.

> That makes no sense, if you use host networking podman doesn't touch anything with ports.

I think in the end, trying out multiple options, I got myself turned around going from `.container` to `.kube` representation.
 
To close the loop, I think my confusion came from the natural feeling with containers of needing to expose the ports that are in use. What made this more confusing was I am trying to run a .kube based quadlet talking to a non-container based PostreSQL that is available over localhost. Understanding the difference of perspective of localhost with each configuration wasn't as obvious being new to trying this.

Thanks @Luap99 for help clearing this up.

Thanks for clearing that up, I was trying to use `podman kube play` to expose port 53 on TCP and UDP with a CoreDNS pod and I didn't want to use `--network bridge` as reported in #19108 since I'm not going to run `podman kube play` myself: I'm trying to use FetchIt to automate my Podman deployments using Kubernetes YAML, and it's `fetchit` that is going to run it.

When I tried putting `hostNetwork: true` in my pod spec, it failed with the same error as this issue. Or course, because I was still trying to map container ports to host ports. Removing the mappings (`ports.hostPort`) solved my problem. It's a shame I cannot comment in #19108 about my solution.
